title: Why Does Going First Sometimes Win and Sometimes Lose?

challenges.stem: Two VPs in a company disagree about product direction. Both have dug in publicly. Neither will back down in meetings. The conflict is paralyzing the team. What's the Kennedy move?

options.text: Create a private channel (1-on-1 with each VP separately) to find a synthesis that lets both claim contribution, then present the joint solution publicly
Escalate to the CEO and force a decision from above
Let the conflict play out — eventually one will yield when they see the team impact
Propose a compromise that splits the difference between both positions

challenges.explanation: This is a chicken game — both VPs can't back down publicly without losing face. Kennedy's move was the back-channel: private communication that lets both sides cooperate without the audience cost. A 50/50 split (option D) often satisfies nobody. Escalation (option B) creates a loser. Waiting (option C) lets the crash happen. The back-channel changes the game from 'who yields publicly' to 'who gets credit for the synthesis.'

challenges.stem: Japanese nemawashi involves consulting every stakeholder privately before any formal meeting. The person who proposes in the meeting has already secured agreement. What force gives the last-mover power here?

options.text: Uncertainty — by gathering everyone's positions first, the last-mover eliminates uncertainty and constructs a proposal no one can object to
Power — the person doing nemawashi has more organizational authority to consult widely
Trust — the slow, personal approach builds deeper trust than rapid first-movers
Fear — stakeholders are afraid to object in the formal meeting after private agreement

challenges.explanation: Nemawashi is fundamentally about information advantage: the last-mover knows everyone's position, red lines, and preferences. They use this to construct a proposal that's already pre-approved. It's not about authority (anyone can do nemawashi), trust (the relationships are instrumental), or fear (people could still object). It's about eliminating uncertainty — the last-mover operates with complete information while first-movers operate blind.

pairs.back: Exists when the game rewards commitment, framing, and lock-in. Setting standards, defining markets, establishing norms. You win by making others respond to YOUR frame.
Exists when the game rewards information and adaptation. Complex negotiations, uncertain markets, multi-stakeholder decisions. You win by knowing everyone's position before acting.
Two players driving at each other — whoever swerves is 'weak' but if neither swerves, both crash. Commitment becomes both weapon and trap.
Refusing the binary (invade or accept), introducing a graduated response that shifts the next move to the opponent and creates time for face-saving resolution.

pairs.left: Startup launching a product in a brand-new category
pairs.right: First-mover advantage — framing the category and capturing network effects early

pairs.left: Amazon entering markets after others prove demand
pairs.right: Last-mover advantage — learning from first-movers' mistakes, entering with superior execution

pairs.left: Two countries in a tariff escalation war
pairs.right: Chicken game — both lose from escalation but neither wants to be seen yielding first

pairs.left: Doing private 1-on-1s before a big team proposal
pairs.right: Nemawashi — eliminating uncertainty by gathering positions before committing publicly

pairs.left: CEO publicly announcing a pivot before securing board buy-in
pairs.right: First-mover gamble — framing power but risks being opposed publicly with no exit

before.label: Speed is always power
before.text: I need to move first — set the agenda, stake my position, force others to respond. Hesitation is weakness. The first-mover captures the market/conversation/frame.

after.label: Timing is strategic
after.text: I diagnose the game first. Does it reward commitment (go first) or information (go last)? In chicken dynamics, I look for the third option. I never let 'appearing strong' override the question of what actually produces the best outcome.
